perturbation.original_text:
1.  Confidential Information
1.1 For the purposes of this Agreement, Confidential Information means any data or i n- formation that is propr ietary to or posses sed by a Party and not generally known to the public or that has not yet been revealed, whether in tangible or intangible form, whe n- ever and however disclosed, including, but not limited to: (i) any scientific or technical information, inv ention, design, process, procedure, formula, improvement, technology or method; The European IPR Helpdesk | P age 4 of 9 (ii) any concepts, samples, reports, data, know -how, works -in-progress, designs, drawings, photographs, development tools, specifications, software pro- grams , source code, object code, flow charts, and databases; (iii) any marketing strategies, plans, financial information, or projections, oper a- tions, sales estimates, business plans and performance results relating to the Party’s past, present or future business activities, or those of its affiliates, subsidiaries and affiliated companies; (iv) trade secrets; plans for products or services, and customer or supplier lists; (v) any other information that should reasonably be recogni sed as Confidential Information by the Parties.
perturbation.changed_text:
1.  Confidential Information
1.1 For the purposes of this Agreement, Confidential Information means any data or i n- formation that is propr ietary to or posses sed by a Party and not generally known to the public or that has not yet been revealed, whether in tangible or intangible form, whe n- ever and however disclosed, including, but not limited to: (i) any scientific or technical information, inv ention, design, process, procedure, formula, improvement, technology or method; The European IPR Helpdesk | P age 4 of 9 (ii) any concepts, samples, reports, data, know -how, works -in-progress, designs, drawings, photographs, development tools, specifications, software pro- grams , source code, object code, flow charts, and databases; (iii) any marketing strategies, plans, financial information, or projections, oper a- tions, sales estimates, business plans and performance results relating to the Party’s past, present or future business activities, or those of its affiliates, subsidiaries and affiliated companies; (iv) trade secrets; plans for products or services, and customer or supplier lists; (v) any other information that the disclosing party deems confidential.
perturbation.explanation: The original text defines confidential information using an objective standard (what 'should reasonably be recognised'). The modified text introduces a subjective standard ('deems confidential'), which broadens the scope of what can be considered confidential, potentially conflicting with trade secret law.
perturbation.law_explanation: The UTSA and similar state laws define trade secrets with specific criteria (e.g., deriving economic value from not being generally known). The modified contract language, by allowing the disclosing party to unilaterally 'deem' any information confidential, could protect information that doesn't meet the statutory definition of a trade secret, potentially hindering legitimate competition and conflicting with the intent of trade secret law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 404 | Berkman Klein Center Skip to the main content Page Cannot Be Found Brave traveler, your journey on our site has brought you to an abrupt dead end. Perhaps you wish to explore one of these other options? About Us Get Involved Events Community Publications Projects & Tools Last updated Mar 27, 2024
perturbation.justification: The modified contract introduces a subjective standard for defining confidential information ('any other information that the disclosing party deems confidential'), while the original text used an objective standard ('should reasonably be recognised'). This shift could allow a party to unilaterally designate information as confidential, even if it doesn't meet the legal criteria for trade secrets, potentially conflicting with trade secret law designed to protect legitimate competitive activity. Scraped Law Snippet 2 is not useful, however, the Economic Espionage Act of 1996 snippet points to the existence of laws governing trade secrets, which the change undermines.

perturbation.original_text: 1.6 Notwithstanding the aforementioned , Confidential Information shall exclude info r- mation that : (i) is already in the public domain at the time o f disclosure by the Disclosing Pa r- ty to the Receiving Party or thereafter enters the public domain without any breach of the terms of this Agreement ; (ii) was already known by the Receiving Party before the moment of disclosure (under evidence of reasonable proof or written record of such disclosure); The European IPR Helpdesk | P age 5 of 9 (iii) is subsequently communicated to the Receiving Party without any obligation of confidence from a third party who is in lawful possession thereof and u n- der no obligation of confidence to the Disclosing Party; (iv) becomes publicly available by other means than a breach of the confidentia l- ity obligations by the Receiving Party (not through fault or failure to act by the Receiving Party) ; (v) is or has been developed independently by employees, consultants or agents of th e Receiving Party (proved by reasonable means) without violation of the terms of this Agreement or reference or access to any Confidential Info r- mation pertaining to the Parties .
perturbation.changed_text: 1.6 Notwithstanding the aforementioned , Confidential Information shall exclude info r- mation that : (i) is already in the public domain at the time o f disclosure by the Disclosing Pa r- ty to the Receiving Party or thereafter enters the public domain without any breach of the terms of this Agreement ; (ii) was already known by the Receiving Party before the moment of disclosure (without regard to proof or record of such prior knowledge); The European IPR Helpdesk | P age 5 of 9 (iii) is subsequently communicated to the Receiving Party without any obligation of confidence from a third party who is in lawful possession thereof and u n- der no obligation of confidence to the Disclosing Party; (iv) becomes publicly available by other means than a breach of the confidentia l- ity obligations by the Receiving Party (not through fault or failure to act by the Receiving Party) ; (v) is or has been developed independently by employees, consultants or agents of th e Receiving Party (proved by reasonable means) without violation of the terms of this Agreement or reference or access to any Confidential Info r- mation pertaining to the Parties .
perturbation.explanation: By removing the requirement for 'reasonable proof or written record' of prior knowledge, the modified text weakens the protection of confidential information. This could allow the Receiving Party to claim prior knowledge without providing evidence, potentially misappropriating trade secrets.
perturbation.law_explanation: The UTSA protects information that is not generally known or readily ascertainable. Removing the evidentiary requirement for prior knowledge makes it easier to argue that information was already known, weakening the protection afforded to trade secrets under the UTSA and increasing the risk of trade secret misappropriation. The UTSA requires proving that information is not readily ascertainable, and this modification hinders that protection.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 404 | Berkman Klein Center Skip to the main content Page Cannot Be Found Brave traveler, your journey on our site has brought you to an abrupt dead end. Perhaps you wish to explore one of these other options? About Us Get Involved Events Community Publications Projects & Tools Last updated Mar 27, 2024
perturbation.justification: The original contract text required 'reasonable proof or written record' to demonstrate prior knowledge of confidential information. The changed text removes this evidentiary requirement. The Law Explanation states that the UTSA (Uniform Trade Secrets Act) protects information not readily ascertainable and that removing the evidentiary requirement weakens the protection afforded to trade secrets. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 does not directly address the UTSA, the Law Explanation explicitly links the change to a weakening of trade secret protection under the UTSA. Scraped Law Snippet 2 is a dead end. Therefore, removing the 'reasonable proof or written record' requirement directly contradicts the intent of trade secret protection laws, as it makes it more difficult to prove that information was not previously known, which is crucial for maintaining its status as a trade secret.

perturbation.original_text: 3.6 All Confidential Information disclosed under this Agreement shall be and remain the property of the Disclosing Party and nothing contained in this Agreement shal l be co n- strued as granting or conferring any rights to such Confidential Information on the other Party. Principally, nothing in this Agreement shall be deemed to grant to the Receiving Party a licence expressly or by implication under any patent, copyrigh t or other intelle c- tual property right. The Receiving Party hereby acknowledges and confirms that all exis t- ing and future intellectual property rights related to the Confidential Inf ormation are exclusive titles of the Disclosing Party. For the sake of cla rity based in reciprocity and good faith of the Parties, the Receiving Party will not apply for or obtain any intellectual proper ty protection in respect of the Confidential Information received. Likewise any modifications and improvements thereof by the R eceiving Party shall be the sole prope r- ty of the Disclosing Party .
perturbation.changed_text: 3.6 All Confidential Information disclosed under this Agreement shall be and remain the property of the Disclosing Party and nothing contained in this Agreement shal l be co n- strued as granting or conferring any rights to such Confidential Information on the other Party. Principally, nothing in this Agreement shall be deemed to grant to the Receiving Party a licence expressly or by implication under any patent, copyrigh t or other intelle c- tual property right. The Receiving Party hereby acknowledges and confirms that all exis t- ing and future intellectual property rights related to the Confidential Inf ormation are exclusive titles of the Disclosing Party. For the sake of cla rity based in reciprocity and good faith of the Parties, the Receiving Party will not apply for or obtain any intellectual proper ty protection in respect of the Confidential Information received. Likewise any modifications and improvements thereof by the R eceiving Party shall be jointly owned by both the disclosing and receiving party..
perturbation.explanation: The original text states that any modifications and improvements to the confidential information by the Receiving Party shall be the sole property of the Disclosing Party. The modified text changes this to joint ownership. This creates ambiguity regarding ownership of derivative works and could conflict with copyright law's treatment of derivative works.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Copyright Act governs ownership of derivative works. While parties can contractually agree on ownership, the default rule is that the copyright in a derivative work is owned by the author of the derivative work, subject to any pre-existing copyrights in the underlying work. By stating that modifications are jointly owned without specifying the nature of the contributions or addressing pre-existing copyrights, the modified text creates a potential conflict with the Copyright Act's framework for ownership of derivative works. This is especially relevant since section 3.6 previously clearly stated the disclosing party was the sole owner of such derivatives, so now this creates an inconsistency.

perturbation.justification: The original contract text clearly assigns sole ownership of modifications and improvements to the Disclosing Party. The changed text alters this to joint ownership, potentially conflicting with the default copyright ownership rules regarding derivative works, as explained by the LLM. While the scraped snippets themselves are unintelligible, the LLM explanation points out that the Copyright Act dictates that derivative works are owned by the author, subject to pre-existing copyrights. The change from sole to joint ownership, therefore, creates an inconsistency and potential contradiction with established copyright principles, particularly given that section 3.6 previously assigned sole ownership. This aligns with the 'Misaligned Terminology - Legal Contradiction' definition, where the changed terminology deviates from legally recognized definitions regarding derivative work ownership.

perturbation.original_text: 4.2 Applicable Law and Jurisdiction This Agreement shall be construed and interpreted by the laws of [choose the applicable law]. The court of [choose the jurisdiction to settle disputes ] shall have jurisdiction.
perturbation.changed_text: 4.2 Applicable Law and Jurisdiction This Agreement shall be governed by the laws of Wakanda. The jurisdiction for any disputes arising under this agreement will be decided by a coin flip.
perturbation.explanation: Choosing a fictional jurisdiction and a completely arbitrary method of dispute resolution renders the clause unenforceable. If Wakanda has no established legal system recognized by the adjudicating body, then the contract is rendered unenforceable.
perturbation.law_explanation: Choice-of-law provisions must choose a jurisdiction with a reasonable relationship to the contract. Selecting 'Wakanda' makes the contract unenforceable because Wakanda is a fictional country with no recognized legal system to adjudicate the matter. Moreover, the concept of jurisdiction is fundamental to law. Making the decision based on a coin flip defies the concept of finding a reasonable or competent jurisdiction.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Restatement (Second) of Contracts - Wikipedia Jump to content From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia Widely cited American legal treatise The Restatement (Second) of the Law of Contracts is a legal treatise from the second series of the Restatements of the Law , and seeks to inform judges and lawyers about general principles of contract common law. It is one of the best-recognized and frequently cited legal treatises [ 1 ] in all of American jurisprudence . Every first-year law student in the United States is exposed to it, and it is a frequently cited non-binding authority in all of U.S. common law in the areas of contracts and commercial transactions. [ 2 ] The American Law Institute began work on the second edition in 1962 and completed it in 1979; the version in use at present has a copyright year of 1981. [ 3 ] For an explanation of the purpose of a restatement of law, see Restatement of the Law . Use and commentary [ edit ] Legal scholars and jurists have commented extensively on the Restatement, both in contrasting it with aspects of the first Restatement, and in evaluating its influence and effectiveness in reaching its stated objectives. It is in this context of direct review that one can find numerous arguments both favoring and criticizing some aspects of the Restatement as an independent source of legal scholarship. [ 4 ] [ 5 ] [ 6 ] [ 7 ] [ 8 ] Although several sections of the Restatement contained new rules which sometimes contradicted existing law, courts citing these sections have predominantly adopted the Restatement's view, citing them as a court would cite statute or code. [ 9 ] Far more common, however, is the practice of citing the Restatement to clarify generally accepted doctrine in every major area of contract and commercial law. It is in this context of legal research that one can find the Restatement used as direct substantiation and persuasive authority , to validate the arguments and interpretations of individual legal practitioners. [ 10 ] [ 11 ] Legacy [ edit ] Although the Restatement of Contracts is still an influential academic work, certain aspects have been superseded in everyday legal practice by the Uniform Commercial Code . Specifically, the UCC has replaced the Restatement (Second) of Contracts in regard to the sale of goods. The Restatement (Second) of Contracts remains the unofficial authority for aspects of contract law which find their genesis in the common law principles of the United States and, previously, England. [ citation needed ] See also [ edit ] Arthur Linton Corbin , first reporter Corpus Juris Secundum References [ edit ] ^ Acceptable citation format under the Bluebook : "Restatement (Second) of Contracts § ___ (Am. L. Inst. 1981)." The Bluebook: A Uniform System of Citation § 12.8.5 (Columbia Law Review Ass'n et al. eds., 17th ed. 2000) ^ Bauman, Richard (2002). Ideology and Community in the First Wave of Critical Legal Studies . Toronto: University of Toronto Press. ISBN 0-8020-8341-2 . ^ Beale, Hugh (2002). Contract Law: Casebooks for the Common Law of Europe . Oxford: Hart Pub. ISBN 978-1-84113-237-2 . ^ Interpretation and Legal Effect in the Second "Restatement of Contracts" Robert Braucher Columbia Law Review , Vol. 81, No. 1 (Jan., 1981), pp. 13-18 ^ Jacobs, Legal Realism or Legal Fiction? Impracticability Under the Restatement (Second) of Contracts , 87 Com. L.J. 289 (1982). ^ Braucher, Offer and Acceptance in the Second Restatement , 74 Yale L.J. 302 (1964). ^ Kelly, The Codification of Contract Law in the Twentieth Century , 88 Dick. L. Rev. 289 (1984). ^ Keyes, The Restatement (Second): Its Misleading Quality and a Proposal for Its Amelioration , 13 Pepp. L. Rev. 23 (1985). ^ Gregory E. Maggs, Ipse Dixit: The Restatement (Second) of Contracts and the Modern Development of Contract Law , 66 Geo. Wash. L. Rev. 508 (1998). ^ Restitution in the Second "Restatement of Contracts"Joseph M. Perillo Columbia Law Review , Vol. 81, No. 1 (Jan., 1981), pp. 37-51 ^ Contracts:
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces a fictional jurisdiction (Wakanda) and an arbitrary method of dispute resolution (coin flip), directly contradicting the fundamental legal requirement of establishing a reasonable and competent jurisdiction for dispute resolution. The Law Explanation states that choice-of-law provisions must choose a jurisdiction with a reasonable relationship to the contract, which Wakanda clearly does not satisfy. The Restatement (Second) of Contracts, as mentioned in Scraped Law Snippet 2, is a widely cited treatise on contract law. Although the snippet is general, the Law Explanation supports that the change would not be enforceable.
